Meta Platforms: Lobbying, Dark Money & the App Store Accountability Act

A comprehensive OSINT investigation into corporate influence, astroturf advocacy, and dark money networks

OSINT Research Product — All Sources Are Public Records

Compiled: 2026-03-12  |  Status: Active Investigation

Executive Summary Headwaters Strategies National Lobbying Digital Childhood Alliance Arabella Network Campaign Contributions State Legislation Strategic Analysis Evidence Summary Sources

I. Executive Summary

Meta Platforms has built a multi-layered influence operation to advance age verification legislation that shifts compliance burdens from social media platforms (its own products) to app store operators (Apple and Google). This operation spans direct lobbying ($26.3M federal in 2025[1]), covert funding of a nominally independent advocacy group (Digital Childhood Alliance[13]), and personnel connections to one of the largest dark money networks in the United States (Arabella Advisors[2]).

The investigation has established 17 confirmed findings, four structurally possible but unproven pathways, and 11 pending investigation threads—all derived exclusively from public records including IRS 990 filings, state lobbying disclosures, campaign finance databases, corporate registries, and archived web content.

II. Headwaters Strategies — Meta’s Colorado Lobbying Firm

Conflict of Interest: The Eichberg Dual Role

Adam Eichberg simultaneously holds two positions that create a structural conflict of interest:

  • Co-founder & Principal of Headwaters Strategies—Meta’s Colorado lobbying firm ($338,500 received from Meta)[6]
  • Board Chair of the New Venture Fund (NVF)—the flagship 501(c)(3) of the Arabella Advisors dark money network, with $669M in annual revenue[23]
  • Founding Board Member of the Windward Fund—another Arabella entity with $311M in revenue[36]

The same person who receives Meta’s lobbying payments oversees the governance of the nation’s largest dark money pass-through entity. Whether this overlap is coincidental or instrumental is a central unresolved question.

FY 2023–2024$107,000 (Meta was 3rd largest client)
Bills LobbiedSB26-051, SB25-086, HB25-1287 — position: “Monitoring” and “Amending”[7]

IV. Digital Childhood Alliance — Astroturf Operation

Meta covertly funds DCA, a 501(c)(4) advocacy group that presents itself as a grassroots coalition of conservative child safety organizations.[13]

Organizational Profile

FieldValue
Tax Status501(c)(4)—no legal obligation to disclose donors
LocationWashington, DC[46]
Domain Registered2024-12-18 (GoDaddy, privacy-protected, 4-year registration through 2028)[25]
Website Live2024-12-19 (fully formed, professionally designed)[26]
Public Launch~February 28, 2025[27]
EINNot publicly identified; no Form 990 filed yet (first due ~May 2026)
Incorporation RecordNot found in CO, DC, DE, VA, or any searched state registry

Leadership — NCOSE and DOJ Antitrust Origins

NameTitleBackground
Casey StefanskiExecutive Director10 years at NCOSE (Senior Dir. Global Partnerships)[29]
Dawn HawkinsChairCEO, National Center on Sexual Exploitation (dual role)[27]
Melissa McKayBoard PresidentUtah mother of five, DCI founder, #FixAppRatings activist since 2019[47]
John ReadSenior Policy Advisor30 years at DOJ Antitrust Division—investigated app stores and Big Tech[30]

Funding Exposure Timeline

April 2025 — Louisiana Sen. Jay Morris directly questioned Stefanski about tech funding at Senate Finance Committee hearing. Stefanski “squirmed, deflected and claimed she ‘didn’t feel comfortable’ answering.” When pressed for yes/no, admitted receiving tech company funding but refused to name companies.[45][12]
July 2025 — Bloomberg reporters exposed Meta as DCA funder.[13]
December 2025 — Brian Lenney op-ed in Deseret News detailed Meta’s manipulation of DCA.[12]

Coalition Members (confirmed)

  • National Center on Sexual Exploitation (NCOSE)
  • The Heritage Foundation
  • Institute for Family Studies
  • Ethics and Public Policy Center
  • Moms for Liberty
  • Protect Young Eyes
  • 45+ additional organizations[32]

Strategic Function

DCA exclusively targets Apple and Google app stores—never Meta, Facebook, or Instagram. Every blog post, testimony, and press release frames app stores as the problem.[25][28] This perfectly serves Meta’s interest: if ASAA becomes law, age verification responsibility falls on Apple/Google, not on social media platforms.

DCA vs. DCI — Shared Infrastructure

FeatureDCA (lobbying arm)DCI (research arm)
Tax Status501(c)(4)501(c)(3)
RegistrarGoDaddyGoDaddy
Domain Created2024-12-182025-06-13
Registration Length4 years (through 2028)1 year
CDNCloudflareCloudflare
EmailMicrosoft 365Microsoft 365
Email MarketingElastic EmailElastic Email
Claimed FundingAdmits tech funding; won’t name companies“Does not accept major tech platform funding”

DCA registered 6 months before DCI—unusual, as typically the research/education arm (c)(3) precedes the lobbying arm (c)(4). This reverse order suggests DCA was the primary objective.[25]

Wayback Machine Analysis (100+ snapshots)

  • First snapshot: 2024-12-19 03:51 UTC—one day after domain registration[26]
  • Site launched fully formed with professional design, statistics, and testimonials
  • Heritage Foundation and NCOSE staff featured prominently from day one
  • No funder disclosures ever existed on any version of the site[25]
  • Every blog post targets Apple/Google—Meta is never mentioned or criticized
  • Cloudflare intermittently blocks archival (403 errors on multiple dates)

V. Arabella Advisors Dark Money Network

The Network

Four entities sharing 1828 L Street NW, Washington, DC 20036, all managed by Arabella Advisors (now Sunflower Services):[37]

EntitySuiteTypeRevenueAssets
New Venture Fund300-A501(c)(3)$669M$768M
Sixteen Thirty Fund300-B501(c)(4)$282M$107M
Windward Fund300-C501(c)(3)$311M$433M
Hopewell Fund300-D501(c)(3)$114M (grants)$173M
Combined——>$1.3 billion>$1.4 billion

NVF Schedule I Analysis — Definitive Negative Finding

Analysis of 2,669 grant recipients across NVF’s 2022–2024 Schedule I filings:[2][5]

  • ZERO grants to ConnectSafely, ICMEC, NCMEC, FOSI, Thorn, Common Sense Media, DCA, DCI, NCOSE, 5Rights, FairPlay, CDT, NetChoice, Chamber of Progress, TechNet, EFF
  • ZERO grants matching digital safety, online protection, or age verification keywords
  • $121.3 million transferred to Sixteen Thirty Fund (c)(4)
  • $20.9 million to North Fund; $8.8 million to Hopewell Fund
  • $150.9 million total in internal Arabella network transfers

Sixteen Thirty Fund Schedule I Analysis — Definitive Negative Finding

Analysis of 306 of 318 grant recipients ($207.8M of $236.5M) from STF’s 2024 filing:[3][4]

  • ZERO grants to any child safety, age verification, digital childhood, or tech policy organization
  • ZERO keyword matches for child, safety, age, digital, online, internet, cyber, tech, or protect
  • Top recipient: America Votes ($27.85M); also Floridians Protecting Freedom ($14M), League of Conservation Voters ($11.8M)
  • Only parent-related grant: ParentsTogether Action ($30K)—focused on economic parenting issues, NOT tech/online safety

Combined Finding

Across 2,975 grants totaling approximately $1.5 billion from both NVF and STF, zero dollars went to any organization related to child online safety, age verification, or the App Store Accountability Act.[2][3][4]

Internal Transfer Map (2024)

NVF ──$121.3M──→ Sixteen Thirty Fund ──$6.8M──→ North Fund NVF ──$8.8M────→ Hopewell Fund NVF ──$20.9M───→ North Fund STF ──$162K────→ NVF (reverse) STF ──$1.4M────→ Hopewell Fund STF ──$6.8M────→ North Fund

The bidirectional NVF ↔ STF transfers ($121.3M one way, $162K back) confirm these entities operate as a unified financial network.[2][4]

The Opaque Pipeline — Structurally Possible, Unproven

Meta Platforms, Inc. │ ├──[CONFIRMED]──→ Headwaters Strategies ($338,500 CO lobbying) │ │ │ └── Adam Eichberg ──→ NVF Board Chair │ │ │ [CONFIRMED: $121.3M transfers] │ │ │ ▼ │ Sixteen Thirty Fund (c)(4) │ [Donors NOT disclosed] │ [$236.5M in grants, 318 recipients] │ [ANALYZED: ZERO child safety grants] │ │ │ [RULED OUT via Schedule I] │ [Remaining paths: fiscal sponsorship, │ non-grant payments, lobbying expenditures] │ ├──[CONFIRMED]──→ Digital Childhood Alliance (direct funding, per Bloomberg) │ ├──[CONFIRMED]──→ Pelican State Partners (LA lobbying) │ ├──[CONFIRMED]──→ Super PACs ($65M CA, ATEP) │ └──[CONFIRMED]──→ 86+ federal lobbyists ($26.3M)

The Schedule I grant mechanism is ruled out as the pathway for Meta → Arabella → DCA funding. If Meta funds flow through the Arabella network, they would have to travel via:

  1. Fiscal sponsorship—DCA operating as a “sponsored project” within an Arabella entity (would not appear on Schedule I)
  2. Non-grant payments—consulting fees, service contracts, or management fees
  3. NVF lobbying expenditures—NVF’s $36.7M in lobbying expenditures (Part IX) could support advocacy without being classified as grants[5]
  4. Other Arabella entities—Windward Fund ($311M), Hopewell Fund ($114M), and North Fund ($27.7M from NVF+STF) not yet analyzed

VI. Campaign Contributions — CO TRACER Analysis

Analysis of Colorado TRACER bulk data (132MB across 3 CSV files, 2024–2026) for campaign contributions connecting investigation targets to ASAA bill sponsors.[8]

Meta Employee → ASAA Sponsor Contributions

DateContributorEmployerAmountRecipient
2025-06-02Jake LevineMeta (Product Manager, LA)$450Matt Ball for Colorado
2025-06-02Jake LevineMeta$725Ball for All Leadership Fund
2025-03-24Kyle GardnerGoogle (Policy Manager)$450Matt Ball for Colorado
2025-06-14James RosenthalPinterest (Attorney)$450Matt Ball for Colorado

Key Finding: A Meta Product Manager contributed $1,175 to ASAA sponsor Matt Ball’s campaign apparatus on the same day (June 2, 2025). Matt Ball sponsors SB26-051 (Age Attestation on Computing Devices), one of three ASAA-model bills in Colorado.[8]

A Google Policy Manager also contributed $450 to the same legislator. Multiple tech company employees from companies directly affected by ASAA targeted the same bill sponsor.

Headwaters Staff — No ASAA-Sponsor Contributions

  • Adam Eichberg: 21 contributions / $10,176 (2023–2025)—broadly Democratic, $1,776 to Bennet for Governor. No contributions to Ball or Paschal.[8]
  • Will Coyne: 36 contributions / $10,035 (2023–2025)—similar pattern. No contributions to Ball or Paschal.[8]
  • Both list “Headwaters Strategies” as employer; pattern is standard lobbyist relationship-building at $450 maximum contribution limit

No contributions from Meta PAC, Facebook PAC, or any Meta corporate entity to either Ball or Paschal found in Colorado TRACER data.[8]

VII. State Legislative Outcomes

Enacted ASAA Laws

StateBillSignedEffectiveKey Details
UtahSB-142[16]Mar 26, 2025ImmediateFirst in nation; McKay provided key testimony[19][50]
TexasSB 2420[20]May 2025Jan 1, 2026Paused by federal judge, Dec 2025 (NetChoice lawsuit)
LouisianaHB-570[22]Jun 30, 2025Jul 1, 2026Unanimous (99-0, 39-0); Morris/Stefanski confrontation[49]

Louisiana HB-570 — The Morris-Stefanski Exchange

At the Senate Commerce Committee hearing, Sen. Jay Morris (R-LA) directly confronted DCA Executive Director Casey Stefanski about funding:[45][12]

  • Morris asked if tech companies fund DCA
  • Stefanski “squirmed, deflected and claimed she ‘didn’t feel comfortable’ answering”
  • When pressed for a yes/no, admitted receiving tech company funding but refused to name companies
  • Identified largest donor as “the father of our founder” (Melissa McKay)
  • Confirmed DCA is a 501(c)(4) with no donor disclosure obligation
  • Acknowledged Meta’s support of the bill; DCA “happy” about it

The bill passed unanimously at every stage—99-0 House, 39-0 Senate, 98-0 and 38-0 in conference. Zero “no” votes.[22]

Chamber of ProgressOpposeFiled opposition letter against LA HB-570
ACT | The App AssociationOpposePublished detailed analysis of Meta’s lobbying campaign[11]
CCIAOpposeUrged caution on privacy risks

VIII. Strategic Analysis

Why Meta Funds ASAA

The App Store Accountability Act requires app stores (Apple, Google) to verify user ages before downloads. It does not require social media platforms to verify ages within their own apps. If ASAA becomes national law:

  1. Apple and Google bear the compliance cost of age verification infrastructure
  2. Meta’s apps (Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, Threads) face no direct age-check mandate
  3. Parents’ regulatory complaints shift from “Meta let my child access harmful content” to “Apple/Google failed to verify my child’s age”
  4. Meta can claim it supports child safety while its competitors absorb the regulatory burden

Why Use Dark Money

Meta’s direct lobbying is publicly disclosed and widely criticized. By funding DCA through opaque channels:

  1. ASAA appears to have grassroots conservative support rather than Big Tech backing
  2. The conservative coalition (Heritage Foundation, Moms for Liberty, NCOSE) provides bipartisan credibility
  3. A “concerned Utah mom” (McKay) provides the human face[47]
  4. A former DOJ antitrust prosecutor (Read) provides legal credibility and insider knowledge[30]
  5. As a 501(c)(4), DCA has no legal obligation to disclose Meta’s funding
  6. The Arabella network structure, if used, adds a further layer of untraceable pass-through funding

Why the Eichberg Connection Matters

Adam Eichberg’s dual role is not necessarily illegal, but it creates a structural conflict of interest:

  • As Headwaters co-founder, he lobbies on behalf of Meta in Colorado[6]
  • As NVF Board Chair, he governs an organization that transfers $121M+ annually to a 501(c)(4) with complete donor opacity[23]
  • The same person who receives Meta’s lobbying payments oversees the governance of the nation’s largest dark money pass-through entity
  • Whether this overlap is coincidental or instrumental is the central unresolved question

IX. Evidence Summary

Confirmed Proven Findings

  1. Meta funds DCA—confirmed by Bloomberg, partially admitted by Stefanski under oath[13][45]
  2. Meta deployed 86+ lobbyists across 45 states for ASAA and related campaigns[10][11]
  3. Meta spent $26.3M on federal lobbying in 2025 (all-time record)[1][10]
  4. Meta paid Headwaters Strategies $338,500 for Colorado lobbying (2019–2026)[6]
  5. Adam Eichberg simultaneously leads Meta’s CO lobbying firm and chairs the NVF board[23][24]
  6. NVF transfers $121.3M to Sixteen Thirty Fund (c)(4) annually[2][5]
  7. NVF does NOT directly fund any child safety or tech policy organizations via Schedule I[2]
  8. DCA and DCI share infrastructure—same registrar, CDN, email, and marketing platform[25]
  9. Pelican State Partners represents Meta as a lobbying client in Louisiana[14]
  10. DCA leadership comes from NCOSE—3 of 4 senior staff have NCOSE connections[29][27]
  11. DCA’s John Read spent 30 years at DOJ Antitrust investigating app stores[30]
  12. ASAA has passed in 3 states (UT, LA, TX) with Texas paused by a federal judge[16][20][22]
  13. Sixteen Thirty Fund does NOT fund any child safety or tech policy organizations via Schedule I[3][4]
  14. Combined NVF + STF: 2,975 grants (~$1.5B) with ZERO child safety/tech policy funding[2][3]
  15. A Meta employee (Jake Levine) contributed $1,175 to ASAA sponsor Matt Ball[8]
  16. A Google Policy Manager (Kyle Gardner) also contributed $450 to Matt Ball[8]
  17. Eichberg and Coyne did NOT contribute to ASAA bill sponsors despite $20K+ combined political giving[8]

Unproven Structurally Possible

  1. Meta funds flow through Arabella network via non-grant mechanisms: fiscal sponsorship, consulting fees, or lobbying expenditures (Schedule I pathway now ruled out)
  2. DCA operates as a fiscal sponsorship under another 501(c)(4) (no incorporation record found)
  3. NVF’s $36.7M in lobbying expenditures support age verification advocacy[5]
  4. Jake Levine’s contribution to Matt Ball was coordinated by Meta’s government affairs team

Pending Not Yet Investigated

  1. FOIA responses due 2026-03-13 (CO SOS) and 2026-03-14 (CO AG, LA Ethics)
  2. DCA incorporation records in DC DLCP
  3. CalAccess individual lobbyist registrations for Meta in California
  4. Windward Fund and Hopewell Fund Schedule I grant recipients
  5. North Fund Schedule I—received $27.7M from NVF + STF, grant recipients not analyzed
  6. OpenCorporates cross-reference for DCA/DCI corporate filings
  7. Meta federal lobbying → Arabella payments via OpenSecrets detailed disclosure data
  8. FollowTheMoney.org state-level contribution data for additional states
  9. Meta’s ATEP super PAC ($45M) independent expenditures supporting ASAA sponsors

Compiled: 2026-03-12  |  Classification: Open Source Intelligence

This is an active investigation. Findings are based exclusively on publicly available records including IRS filings, state lobbying disclosures, campaign finance databases, corporate registries, and archived web content. Pending items include FOIA responses, additional Arabella entity Schedule I analyses, and state ethics board filings.

Document generated from 17 research files comprising analysis of 2,975 IRS Schedule I grant records, 132MB of campaign finance data, 100+ Wayback Machine snapshots, and state lobbying disclosure APIs.
